food_subgroup: Mixed rice dish with meat and fermented condiment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Burmese tea leaf rice with mutton. FOODON_00002473 (rice dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Burmese lahpet htamin with mutton.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Burmese rice dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a plate of steamed white rice topped with braised or stir-fried mutton pieces, fermented tea leaf dressing, fried peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, and fried garlic.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: cooked white rice (USDA FoodData Central #168878), cooked mutton/goat meat (USDA FoodData Central #175299), and fermented tea leaf salad components (FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table analogues); weighted by approximate recipe proportions (55% rice, 25% mutton, 10% lahpet dressing, 10% garnishes).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Mutton Tea Leaf Rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA databases. Values estimated from weighted component analysis: cooked white rice (~55% by weight), cooked goat/mutton (~25%), fermented tea leaf dressing (~10%), and mixed garnishes including peanuts, sesame, dried shrimp, and fried garlic (~10%). USDA FoodData Central used for rice and mutton components. FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues used for fermented tea leaf components. All values are estimates and should be validated against laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice dishes — analogous estimate based on steamed white rice GI (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) adjusted for protein and fat co-ingestion from mutton and lahpet components.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Tea Leaf Rice. Steamed white rice has a GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database). The substantial protein (~8.5 g/100g from mutton) and fat (~5.8 g/100g) content of this mixed dish attenuates the glycemic response. An estimated GI of 56 is applied, consistent with published evidence that protein and fat co-ingestion with rice reduces the effective GI of the meal by 10–15 units (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 81.7) / 100 = 45.8, rounded to 45.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce, dried shrimp, and fermented tea leaf dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides an estimated 1,178 mg sodium, representing approximately 49–59% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1,500–2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals); patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and dried shrimp, or limit portion size to half a plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (estimated GI 56) and high carbohydrate load from white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 46 per serving is high for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is essential, and substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion by one-third can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton and sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mutton is a relatively saturated-fat-rich red meat (~2.1 g saturated fat per 100 g of dish), and the high sodium from fermented condiments elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat frequency, choose leaner cuts, and request minimal fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Tea Leaf Rice (ဆိတ်လက်ထမင်း in Burmese) is a Burmese-origin rice dish served in Singapore, consisting of steamed white rice topped with braised or stir-fried mutton (goat meat) pieces and dressed with lahpet — a distinctively Burmese preparation of fermented and seasoned tea leaves. The dish is garnished with fried peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, crispy fried garlic, dried shrimp, and a drizzle of fish sauce and lime juice, creating a complex interplay of savoury, umami, slightly bitter, and nutty flavours. The fermented tea leaf component (lahpet) is a culturally significant ingredient in Burmese cuisine, traditionally consumed as both a snack and a condiment, and imparts a characteristic earthy bitterness and deep umami note. The mutton contributes a robust, slightly gamey flavour and provides a meaningful source of protein (~8.5 g per 100 g) and iron (~1.4 mg per 100 g). Nutritionally, the dish is energy-moderate at approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with a macronutrient profile dominated by carbohydrates from white rice (~21.5 g per 100 g), supplemented by protein from mutton and fat from peanuts, sesame, and cooking oil. The sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1,178 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 56 (medium) reflects the moderating effect of protein and fat on the glycemic response of the white rice base. This dish is halal-suitable and gluten-free but contains peanuts, sesame, and shellfish allergens. It is typically served as a single-plate lunch or dinner meal in Burmese restaurants and cafes in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Mutton Tea Leaf Rice exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). All nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice and cooked goat meat, supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues for fermented tea leaf components. The GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI adjusted for protein and fat co-ingestion. Confidence is therefore low and laboratory validation is recommended.
notes: 1. Cuisine correction: The input JSON listed cuisine as 'Italian' with tags 'Italian, Pizza, Coffee, Toast', which is clearly erroneous for a Burmese dish. The cuisine has been corrected to 'Burmese' based on the dish name and Burmese-script food name (ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထမင်း). The restaurant 'The Light Cafe - High Street Plaza' appears to serve a multi-cuisine menu including Burmese items. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis and carry low confidence; laboratory analysis is strongly recommended before clinical use. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimation, and no reliable value is available from approved sources for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 4. Halal status is assessed as likely true given mutton is a halal-permissible meat and the dish does not contain pork or alcohol; however, restaurant-level halal certification has not been verified. 5. The GI of 56 places this dish in the 'medium' GI category per the validator threshold (56–69 = medium). Glycemic load per serving = (56 × 81.7 g carbohydrate per 380 g serving) / 100 = 45.8, reported as 45.9. 6. All -1 sentinel values: vitamin_d_mcg is -1 (not reported in source database); selenium_mcg is -1 (not reported in source database).
